Figure review

Every rendered figure, in curriculum order, with the caption a student sees. Type any change/improvement in the box under a figure — notes auto-save in this browser. Tick reviewed to track progress. When done, hit Export notes to gather everything into one batch prompt (keyed to FIG number + file) you can paste back to apply all edits at once. Use Dark check to see how figures look in dark mode.

Unit 3 · AOS 1 — Motion

Motion in two dimensionsPass 1 · Big picture · pass1/motion.html
FIG 1.1 pass1/motion.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · Independence of the two motions. A dropped ball and a horizontally launched ball, released together, fall through the same height in the same time — their vertical positions match at every flash, even though one is also travelling sideways.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white strobe-style diagram, landscape. A horizontal tabletop at upper left with a small ball just leaving its right edge, launched horizontally to the right (short horizontal velocity arrow labelled "launched sideways"). Directly below the table edge, a second identical ball released from rest at the same height (no horizontal arrow, labelled "dropped"). Show both balls as a vertical sequence of strobe positions at equal time intervals (at least five flashes each), the flash images getting progressively further apart downward to convey acceleration. CRITICAL: connect each pair of simultaneous flashes with a faint horizontal dashed guide line, so the reader sees the two balls are always at the same height at the same instant. The launched ball's successive positions trace a parabola opening downward-right; the dropped ball's positions lie on a vertical line. Equal horizontal spacing between the launched ball's flashes (constant horizontal velocity); increasing vertical spacing for both (constant downward acceleration g). At every flash draw a single short downward arrow on each ball labelled "g" (identical length on both balls at every height) to stress the force is always downward and constant. Both balls strike a common ground line at lower portion at the final flash, with annotation "land together". Label the constant horizontal steps "equal steps — constant speed" and the growing vertical steps "growing steps — speeding up". Primary labels ≥ 22 pt sans-serif at authoring scale; typeset g in maths italic; "Not drawn to scale." footnote.
FIG 1.2 pass1/motion.html · #fig-1-2
FIG 1.2 · One inward force, three disguises. A ball on a string, a car cornering on a flat road, and a car on a banked track — in every case a single real force points toward the centre and provides the centripetal force, while the velocity points along the tangent.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three square panels in a row, black and white, equal framing and line weights, each a top-or-front view of one circular-motion situation with the centre of the circle marked by a small cross labelled "centre". In every panel: a bold solid arrow from the object toward the centre labelled "net force (centripetal)", and a distinct dashed arrow tangent to the circle at the object's position labelled "velocity", the two arrows clearly at right angles. Panel (a) "Ball on a string": top view, a ball on a circular path with a string drawn from the ball to the centre; the inward arrow lies along the string, labelled "tension". Panel (b) "Car on a flat road": top view of a car on a circular road arc; the inward arrow at the tyres labelled "friction"; small note "no friction → car slides straight on (dashed straight tangent line continuing off the curve)". Panel (c) "Car on a banked track": front-on (head-on) cross-section view of a car sitting on a road surface tilted at angle θ to the horizontal; draw the normal force as an arrow perpendicular to the tilted road surface, then show its two components with thin dashed arrows — a vertical component labelled "balances weight (mg)" and a horizontal component pointing toward the centre labelled "provides centripetal force"; mark the bank angle θ between road and horizontal; small note "at design speed: no friction needed". Across all panels add a footer strip: "There is NO outward force — the only real forces point inward." Primary labels ≥ 22 pt sans-serif; typeset subscripts/θ with maths formatting; identical arrowhead sizes throughout; "Not drawn to scale." footnote.
1 · Projectile motionPass 2 · Core · pass2/projectile-motion.html
FIG 1.1 pass2/projectile-motion.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · Free-body diagram for a block on a frictionless slope inclined at angle θ. The weight mg is resolved into a component mg sinθ down the slope and a component mg cosθ into the slope; the normal force balances only the perpendicular component, so N = mg cosθ.
artwork spec (for reference)
A right-angled wedge with its hypotenuse rising to the right, angle θ marked at the lower-left vertex between the slope and the horizontal. A square block sits on the slope, its centre marked with a dot. From the dot: a solid arrow straight down labelled "mg" (weight); a solid arrow perpendicular to the slope surface, pointing away from it, labelled "N" (normal force), drawn the same length as the mg cosθ component. Dashed construction arrows from the dot show the weight resolved along the slope axes: one dashed arrow down-the-slope labelled "mg sinθ" and one dashed arrow into-the-slope labelled "mg cosθ", forming a right-angled parallelogram with the mg arrow as its diagonal; mark the right angle between the two dashed components and show the angle between mg and mg cosθ equals θ. Set of light dashed axes through the dot, one parallel to the slope, one perpendicular. Black and white, true right angles, the N arrow and the mg cosθ dashed arrow drawn equal in length to show they balance. Typeset θ and all unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
FIG 1.2 pass2/projectile-motion.html · #fig-1-2
FIG 1.2 · The parabolic path of a projectile launched at angle θ above the horizontal. The horizontal velocity component ux is the same at every point; the vertical component falls to zero at the top of the path and reverses on the way down. The path is symmetric about the highest point.
artwork spec (for reference)
A symmetric parabola opening downward, drawn from a launch point on a horizontal ground line, rising to a peak and returning to the ground line. Ground line with a small launch marker at the left end and a landing marker at the right end; mark the horizontal distance between them with a dimension arrow labelled "range R", and a dashed vertical drop-line from the peak to the ground labelled "maximum height H". At the launch point: a single velocity arrow at angle θ above the horizontal labelled "v", with θ marked to the ground line, plus dashed component arrows "u_x = v cosθ" (horizontal) and "u_y = v sinθ" (vertical). At three points along the path — just after launch, at the peak, and just before landing — draw the velocity as a solid tangent arrow with its horizontal and vertical components as dashed arrows: the horizontal component is IDENTICAL in length at all three points; the vertical component is upward and shrinking before the peak, ZERO at the peak (label "v_y = 0 at top, v_x unchanged"), and downward and growing after it. A separate small arrow near the path labelled "a = g, downward" with a note "constant throughout, even at the top". Mark that the launch and landing speeds are equal and the launch and landing angles are equal (θ above, θ below). Black and white, true parabola, equal arrowheads so only shaft length conveys magnitude. Typeset θ and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
FIG 1.3 pass2/projectile-motion.html · #fig-1-3
FIG 1.3 · Comparison of the ideal parabolic path (no air resistance) with the path of the same projectile subject to air resistance. The real path is asymmetric, with a reduced maximum height and range and a steeper descent than ascent.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two paths from the same launch point on a horizontal ground line, both launched at the same angle. Path 1: a symmetric downward parabola drawn as a solid line, labelled "no air resistance (ideal)", reaching a higher peak and a farther landing point; mark its range and peak height with thin dashed reference lines. Path 2: drawn as a dashed line starting along the same initial direction but falling below path 1, reaching a lower and slightly earlier peak, then descending more steeply than it rose, landing at a shorter range; labelled "with air resistance". Annotate path 2 with: "reduced maximum height", "reduced range", and "descent steeper than ascent (asymmetric)". At one point on path 2, draw the velocity arrow (tangent to the path) and an air-resistance arrow pointing in exactly the opposite direction, labelled "air resistance opposes velocity". Black and white, clear distinction between solid and dashed paths, equal aspect so the parabola is true. Typeset unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
2 · Circular motionPass 2 · Core · pass2/circular-motion.html
FIG 2.1 pass2/circular-motion.html · #fig-2-1
FIG 2.1 · A car on a flat circular road. The velocity is tangential; the friction force from the road points toward the centre and supplies the centripetal force. There is no outward force.
artwork spec (for reference)
Top-down (bird’s-eye) view. A dashed circle of radius r with the centre marked by a dot labelled “centre”. A small car drawn as a rectangle sitting on the circle. From the car: a solid arrow drawn tangent to the circle (perpendicular to the radius at that point) labelled “v (velocity, tangential)”; a separate solid arrow along the radius pointing inward toward the centre labelled “friction F (toward centre = F_net)”. Draw the radius as a thin line from centre to car labelled “r”. Add a struck-through (crossed-out) outward arrow in light grey labelled “no centrifugal force” to flag the misconception. Black and white, equal aspect ratio so the circle is circular; tangent arrow exactly 90° to the radius. Typeset any exponents or unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 2.2 pass2/circular-motion.html · #fig-2-2
FIG 2.2 · A vehicle on a frictionless banked track, viewed from behind. The normal force is perpendicular to the surface; its horizontal component supplies the centripetal force toward the centre, and its vertical component balances the weight.
artwork spec (for reference)
Rear (cross-section) view. A flat banked surface drawn as a line tilted by angle θ above the horizontal, with the bank angle θ marked at the lower edge against a dashed horizontal reference line. A car shown as a rectangle resting on the tilted surface. From the car’s centre: a solid arrow straight down labelled “mg (weight)”; a solid arrow perpendicular to the tilted surface labelled “N (normal force)”. Resolve N with thin dashed component arrows: a horizontal one pointing toward the centre of the bend labelled “N sinθ (centripetal)” and a vertical one pointing up labelled “N cosθ (balances mg)”. Mark the centre of the circular path off to the side at the level of the road with a dot and a horizontal dashed line labelled “toward centre”. Show the right angle between N and the surface with a small square. Black and white; angle θ clearly equal where it appears (between surface and horizontal, and between N and the vertical). Typeset exponents and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode.
FIG 2.3 pass2/circular-motion.html · #fig-2-3
FIG 2.3 · Free-body diagrams at the top and bottom of a vertical circle. At the top, T and mg both point toward the centre and add; at the bottom they oppose. The centre of the circle is marked in each case.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two free-body panels side by side for a ball on a string moving in a vertical circle, with the circle and its centre (dot) drawn faintly behind each. LEFT panel “Top of circle”: ball at the top, centre directly below it; two solid arrows from the ball, both pointing DOWN toward the centre — the upper labelled “T (tension)” and “mg (weight)” stacked, with a bracket “F_net = T + mg = mv²/r (toward centre)”. RIGHT panel “Bottom of circle”: ball at the bottom, centre directly above it; one solid arrow UP toward the centre labelled “T (tension)” and one solid arrow DOWN away from the centre labelled “mg (weight)”, with a bracket “F_net = T − mg = mv²/r (toward centre)”. In each panel add a small tangential velocity arrow labelled “v”. Black and white; arrows on a clear vertical line; equal scale between panels. Typeset exponents and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 2.4 pass2/circular-motion.html · #fig-2-4
FIG 2.4 · A satellite modelled as uniform circular motion. The orbital radius r is measured from Earth’s centre, so r = RE + altitude. The velocity is tangential; the acceleration is radial, toward the centre.
artwork spec (for reference)
Earth as a circle with its centre marked by a dot. A small satellite on a dashed circular orbit. At the satellite: a tangential solid arrow labelled “v (tangential)” and a radial solid arrow pointing toward Earth’s centre labelled “a = v²/r (toward centre)”. Mark the centre-to-satellite distance with a thin dimension line labelled “r (from centre)” and separately mark the surface-to-satellite gap labelled “altitude”, with the note “r = R_E + altitude”. Add “Not drawn to scale.” Black and white, equal aspect ratio so circles are circular; the tangent velocity arrow exactly perpendicular to the radius. Typeset exponents and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
3 · Momentum and energyPass 2 · Core · pass2/momentum-energy.html
FIG 3.1 pass2/momentum-energy.html · #fig-3-1
FIG 3.1 · The impulse delivered to the trolley is the area between the force–time graph and the time axis. The trapezium is read as one triangle, one rectangle and one triangle, totalling 12 N s.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only, light gridlines. Vertical axis "force F (N)" from 0 to 50, gridlines every 10 N; horizontal axis "time t (s)" from 0 to 0.45, gridlines every 0.05 s. Plot a trapezoidal pulse: straight line from (0, 0) up to (0.10, 40), horizontal line from (0.10, 40) to (0.30, 40), straight line down from (0.30, 40) to (0.40, 0). Shade the whole enclosed region light grey. Divide the shaded region with two thin dashed vertical lines at t = 0.10 and t = 0.30, and annotate the three parts: left triangle "½ × 0.10 × 40 = 2.0 N s", central rectangle "0.20 × 40 = 8.0 N s", right triangle "½ × 0.10 × 40 = 2.0 N s". Add a total label "impulse = area = 12 N s". No data points. Typeset exponents and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
FIG 3.2 pass2/momentum-energy.html · #fig-3-2
FIG 3.2 · For an ideal spring the force–extension graph is a straight line of gradient k. The elastic potential energy stored at extension x is the shaded triangular area beneath it, Es = ½kx².
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only. Vertical axis "force F (N)" from 0 to 24, gridlines every 4 N; horizontal axis "extension x (m)" from 0 to 0.10, gridlines every 0.02 m. Plot a single straight line from the origin (0, 0) to (0.080, 20), with gradient labelled "gradient = k = 250 N m⁻¹". Shade the triangular region under the line from x = 0 to x = 0.080 light grey, and label it "area = E_s = ½kx² = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J". Add a dashed vertical drop-line at x = 0.080 to the point (0.080, 20), and a dashed horizontal line from (0.080, 20) to the F axis at 20 N labelled "F = kx = 20 N". No data points. Typeset exponents and unit indices with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
Motion in two dimensionsPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos1-motion.html
FIG 1.1 pass3/u3aos1-motion.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · Free-body diagram of a block on an incline, with the weight resolved into components parallel (mg sinθ) and perpendicular (mg cosθ) to the slope, and the normal force N shown equal to the perpendicular component.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white line drawing. Right-angled wedge, slope angle θ marked at the base. Block on the slope. Solid arrows: weight straight down from block centre labelled mg; normal force perpendicular to slope surface labelled N. Dashed construction arrows resolving mg into mg sinθ (down-slope) and mg cosθ (into-slope), with a small right-angle square at the resolution point. Axes drawn lightly along and perpendicular to the slope. No colour, no shading.
FIG 1.2 pass3/u3aos1-motion.html · #fig-1-2
FIG 1.2 · Projectile trajectory showing the velocity resolved into a constant horizontal component and a changing vertical component, with the weight force drawn downward at three points including the apex.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white line drawing. Parabolic path left to right. At launch, mid-ascent and apex, draw the velocity vector (solid) decomposed by dashed lines into horizontal (equal length at all points) and vertical (shrinking to zero at apex) components. At all three points draw a single short solid arrow straight down labelled mg to show the force is always downward. Dashed lighter curve below the main path showing the shorter, steeper-descending trajectory when air resistance acts, labelled "with air resistance". No colour.
FIG 1.3 pass3/u3aos1-motion.html · #fig-1-3
FIG 1.3 · Free-body diagram of a vehicle on a banked track at the design speed: the normal force N perpendicular to the banked surface resolved into N cosθ (vertical, balancing mg) and N sinθ (horizontal, directed toward the centre of the circle).
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white line drawing. Cross-section of a banked road surface tilted at angle θ from horizontal, viewed end-on, with the centre of the circular path to the left. Vehicle box on the bank. Solid arrows: weight mg straight down; normal force N perpendicular to the road surface. Dashed components of N: N cosθ vertical (up), N sinθ horizontal (toward centre, left). Angle θ marked between the road surface and horizontal, and between N and the vertical. A horizontal dashed line indicates the centre direction. No friction arrow. No colour.
FIG 1.4 pass3/u3aos1-motion.html · #fig-1-4
FIG 1.4 · A force–time graph for a collision; the shaded area under the curve equals the impulse delivered, and hence the change in momentum.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white line graph. Horizontal axis labelled "time (s)", vertical axis labelled "force (N)". A triangular-then-flat force pulse: force rises linearly from 0 to a peak, holds briefly, then falls linearly to 0. Region under the curve hatched with diagonal lines and labelled "impulse = area = Δp". Gridlines light. No colour.

Unit 3 · AOS 2 — Fields

Action without contactPass 1 · Big picture · pass1/fields.html
FIG 1.1 pass1/fields.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · One drawing grammar, three fields: the field of a point mass (every line inward — a sink), a positive point charge (every line outward — a source) and a bar magnet (a dipole — out of N, around, into S). Arrowheads give the field direction; the spreading of the lines shows the field weakening with distance.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three equal square panels in a row, black and white, identical framing and equal aspect ratio. Panel (a) "gravitational": filled circle at centre labelled "mass M"; eight straight field lines equally spaced around it, meeting the circle at 90°, arrowhead on every line pointing radially INWARD toward the mass; lines run to the panel edge to suggest infinite extent. Panel (b) "electric": open circle with a plus sign, labelled "charge +Q"; eight straight lines equally spaced, arrowheads pointing radially OUTWARD. Panel (c) "magnetic": horizontal bar magnet, left half labelled "N", right half labelled "S"; eight external field lines only (no internal lines): one axial line from each end face plus three curved lines above and three below, leaving the N face and entering the S face, symmetric about the long axis, arrowheads N→S outside the magnet. All arrowheads identical in size across all panels; only line spacing conveys strength (lines visibly farther apart at greater distance from each source). Panel titles beneath: (a) gravitational, (b) electric, (c) magnetic. Footnote line: "Not drawn to scale." Primary labels ≥ 22 pt at authoring scale, sans-serif; check legibility at one-third scale.
4 · The field ideaPass 2 · Core · pass2/field-idea.html
FIG 1.1 pass2/field-idea.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · Radial fields of point sources. The field of a point mass and the field of a negative point charge have identical inward patterns; only the test-object convention distinguishes them.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three equal panels, black and white. (a) Point mass M (filled circle, labelled M): eight straight radial lines, arrowheads pointing inward toward M, line spacing visibly increasing with distance from M. (b) Point charge +Q (circle with + sign): eight radial lines, arrowheads pointing outward. (c) Point charge −Q (circle with − sign): eight radial lines, arrowheads pointing inward, identical geometry to panel (a). Same number of lines in all panels; identical arrowhead size throughout; labels "(a) mass M", "(b) charge +Q", "(c) charge −Q" centred below each panel.
FIG 1.2 pass2/field-idea.html · #fig-1-2
FIG 1.2 · An electric dipole (left) and a bar magnet (right) produce the same external field pattern, yet the charges can be separated and the poles cannot.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two panels, black and white, equal aspect ratio. Left: charges +Q and −Q (circles with + and − signs) separated horizontally; continuous field lines leaving +Q and entering −Q, including one straight line along the axis between them and symmetric curved lines above and below; arrowheads on every line pointing + to −. Right: bar magnet (rectangle, left half labelled N, right half labelled S) with the identical external line pattern, arrowheads N to S; no field lines drawn inside the magnet (VCE convention). Line density highest near the poles/charges in both panels; identical arrowhead size throughout.
FIG 1.3 pass2/field-idea.html · #fig-1-3
FIG 1.3 · The magnetic field of a straight wire, a single loop and a solenoid. Each is built from the same grip rule; the solenoid's exterior field matches a bar magnet's.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three panels, black and white. (a) Vertical straight wire with current arrow I upward; three concentric circles around the wire in a horizontal plane, radii increasing with growing spacing, arrowheads anticlockwise viewed from above; inset of a right hand gripping the wire, thumb up, labelled "thumb = I, fingers = B". (b) Single circular loop with current direction arrows marked on the wire; field lines threading through the centre, emerging from one face and looping around to the other; faces labelled N and S. (c) Solenoid in cross-section: two rows of wire circles, top row marked • and bottom row marked × (key beside panel: "× current into page · • current out of page"); inside, four parallel equally spaced horizontal field lines, arrowheads left to right; outside, bar-magnet-style return loops; left end labelled S, right end labelled N where lines exit. Identical arrowhead size throughout.
FIG 1.4 pass2/field-idea.html · #fig-1-4
FIG 1.4 · Non-uniform and uniform fields. Earth's radial field is non-uniform, but a laboratory-sized region near the surface is well approximated as uniform — the same idealisation used between parallel plates.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three panels, black and white. (a) Earth as a circle with eight straight radial field lines, arrowheads pointing inward toward the centre, spacing increasing with distance; a small square drawn on the surface marking the zoom region. (b) Magnified view of that square: parallel, equally spaced, vertical field lines with arrowheads pointing down toward a hatched ground line; label "g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹". (c) Two horizontal parallel plates, top labelled +, bottom labelled −; parallel equally spaced vertical field lines between them, arrowheads from + plate to − plate; slight outward curving of the two outermost lines at the plate edges, labelled "edge effect". Identical arrowhead size throughout.
5 · Gravity and orbitsPass 2 · Core · pass2/gravity-orbits.html
FIG 2.1 pass2/gravity-orbits.html · #fig-2-1
FIG 2.1 · Gravitational field strength of Earth against distance from its centre. Outside the surface, g follows the inverse-square law; inside, the formula no longer applies and the field falls to zero at the centre, leaving a sharp corner in the graph at r = RE.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only. Vertical axis "gravitational field strength g (N kg⁻¹)" with ticks at 2.45, 9.81; horizontal axis "distance from Earth's centre r" with ticks at 0, R_E, 2R_E, 3R_E, 4R_E. Segment 1 (0 to R_E): straight line from the origin rising to the point (R_E, 9.81), labelled "inside Earth (uniform-density model)". Segment 2 (R_E onward): inverse-square curve g = GM/r² through (R_E, 9.81), (2R_E, 2.45), (3R_E, 1.09); label the curve "g = GM/r² (applies for r ≥ R_E only)". The two segments meet exactly at (R_E, 9.81) forming a visible corner; mark this point with a dashed drop-line to each axis and the label "surface". Dashed drop-lines also from (2R_E, 2.45) and (3R_E, 1.09). No data points, clean lines, equal arrowhead-free axes. Typeset all exponents and superscript unit indices with maths formatting (e.g. $^{-1}$), never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
FIG 2.2 pass2/gravity-orbits.html · #fig-2-2
FIG 2.2 · The only force acting on an orbiting satellite is the gravitational force, directed toward Earth’s centre — and the satellite pulls back on Earth with a force of equal magnitude, forming a Newton’s third law pair.
artwork spec (for reference)
Earth as a circle with its centre marked by a dot; a small square satellite on a dashed circular orbit path. At the satellite: a tangential velocity arrow labelled "v" and a radial arrow toward Earth's centre labelled "F_on satellite by Earth". At Earth's centre: an arrow of IDENTICAL length pointing toward the satellite labelled "F_on Earth by satellite". All arrowheads identical in size; only shaft length conveys magnitude (the two force arrows equal). Mark the centre-to-centre distance r with a thin double-headed dimension arrow labelled "r (centre to centre)", and separately mark the surface-to-satellite gap labelled "altitude h", with the note "r = R_E + h". Key below the figure: "F_g = gravitational force". Add "Not drawn to scale." Black and white, equal aspect ratio so circles are circular.
FIG 2.3 pass2/gravity-orbits.html · #fig-2-3
FIG 2.3 · The change in gravitational potential energy of a 250 kg satellite moved from r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m to r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m equals the shaded area under the force–distance graph, estimated by counting grid squares.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only, with light gridlines (students must read values). Vertical axis "force (N)" from 0 to 2500, gridlines every 500 N; horizontal axis "distance from Earth's centre r (×10⁶ m)" from 6.5 to 9.5, gridlines every 0.5 ×10⁶ m. Smooth inverse-square curve for a 250 kg satellite through (6.5, 2360), (7.0, 2030), (7.5, 1770), (8.0, 1560), (8.5, 1380), (9.0, 1230), (9.5, 1100). Shade light grey, semi-transparent, the region under the curve between r = 7.0 and r = 9.0, labelled "area = ΔE_g ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J"; dashed drop-lines at r = 7.0 and r = 9.0. Highlight ONE grid square with a darker outline and annotate it "1 square = 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J". No visible data points. Typeset exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode; labels legible at one-third scale.
6 · Electric fieldsPass 2 · Core · pass2/electric-fields.html
FIG 3.1 pass2/electric-fields.html · #fig-3-1
FIG 3.1 · The field of a single positive and a single negative point charge, and the combined fields of like and unlike pairs. The negative charge's inward radial field is the same shape as the gravitational field of a point mass.
artwork spec (for reference)
Four panels, black and white, equal aspect ratio. (a) Isolated charge marked + (small circle) with eight straight radial field lines, arrowheads pointing outward. (b) Isolated charge marked − with the identical eight-line radial pattern, arrowheads pointing inward; small annotation "same shape as g around a point mass". (c) Two equal positive charges: lines curve away from each other with a clear field-free null point midway between the charges; lines never cross or meet. (d) Unlike pair (dipole): lines leave the + charge and curve around to enter the − charge, arrowheads running + to −. Every line carries exactly one arrowhead; arrowhead size identical on all lines; line density visibly higher near each charge.
FIG 3.2 pass2/electric-fields.html · #fig-3-2
FIG 3.2 · The uniform field between oppositely charged parallel plates. Equal spacing of the field lines is the visual statement that the field strength is the same everywhere in the gap.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two horizontal plates in cross-section: upper plate labelled +, lower plate labelled −, both connected to a supply symbol labelled V. Plate separation marked d with a double-headed dimension arrow placed outside the gap. Between the plates: straight, parallel, equally spaced field lines running from the + plate to the − plate, one arrowhead per line, all arrowheads identical. At each plate edge: two faint dashed curved lines annotated "fringing field at edges — ignored; field assumed uniform". In-gap annotation: "E = V/d, same magnitude and direction at every point". Black and white only.
FIG 3.3 pass2/electric-fields.html · #fig-3-3
FIG 3.3 · The electron gun: the front end of every particle accelerator. A heated cathode supplies electrons; the uniform field between cathode and anode does work qV0 on each one.
artwork spec (for reference)
Cross-section of an evacuated tube (label "evacuated tube"). Left: heated filament labelled "heated cathode — electrons released", with a small heater supply. Right: plate anode with central aperture, labelled "anode". Accelerating supply labelled V0 connected with − to the cathode and + to the anode. Between the electrodes: equally spaced field lines pointing from anode to cathode (the field direction), with a separate labelled force arrow on one electron, F = qE, pointing toward the anode (opposite to the field). Dashed beam arrow from cathode through the anode aperture, labelled "electron beam, qV0 = ½mv²". Black and white, all components labelled.
7 · Magnetic fieldsPass 2 · Core · pass2/magnetic-fields.html
FIG 4.1 pass2/magnetic-fields.html · #fig-4-1
FIG 4.1 · The right-hand slap rule: thumb along the motion of positive charge (or conventional current), fingers along the field, palm facing along the force — and the reversal for a negative charge.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two panels, black and white, equal size. Panel 1 ("positive charge or conventional current"): a flat right hand, fingers together, thumb at 90° to the fingers. Three solid labelled arrows: along the thumb "v (positive charge) or I (conventional current)"; along the fingers "B (field)"; emerging perpendicular from the palm "F (force)". Panel 2 ("negative charge"): the same hand and the same v and B arrows, with the solid force arrow replaced by a dashed arrow in the opposite direction labelled "F on a negative charge — reversed". Small annotation under panel 2: "equivalent method: point the thumb against the electron's motion". All arrowheads identical in size; primary labels at least 22 pt; no colour.
FIG 4.2 pass2/magnetic-fields.html · #fig-4-2
FIG 4.2 · An electron in a uniform field into the page: the force always points to the centre, the velocity is always tangent, and the speed never changes.
artwork spec (for reference)
A rectangular region filled with a regular grid of × symbols; key at top right: "× = field into the page". An electron (small filled circle labelled e⁻) enters at the left edge moving right, with an entry velocity arrow labelled v. Its path is a clockwise circular arc curving toward the bottom of the page (for an electron moving right in a field into the page, the initial force is down the page). Mark the circle centre C below the entry point and draw a thin line from C to the electron labelled r. At two further positions on the arc draw a tangential velocity arrow labelled v and a radial arrow toward C labelled F. All velocity arrows identical in length, with the annotation "speed constant". Arrowheads identical in size across all arrows; equal aspect ratio so the arc is a true circle; labels at least 22 pt; black and white.
FIG 4.3 pass2/magnetic-fields.html · #fig-4-3
FIG 4.3 · A wire between magnet poles: maximum force when the current crosses the field at right angles, zero force when the current runs along the field.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two panels, black and white, identical pole geometry. Panel 1: pole faces labelled N (left) and S (right) with evenly spaced horizontal field lines running left to right, arrowheads on every line. Between the poles, the cross-section of a wire perpendicular to the page carrying current out of the page (• at the wire centre, key beneath: "• = current out of the page"). A force arrow on the wire pointing vertically up the page labelled "F = nIlB". Panel 2: same poles and field lines; the wire now lies horizontally, parallel to the field, with a current arrow along it pointing from the N side toward the S side labelled I, and the annotation "F = 0 — current parallel to B". Identical line weights and arrowhead sizes in both panels; labels at least 22 pt.
8 · Motors &amp; acceleratorsPass 2 · Core · pass2/machines.html
FIG 5.1 pass2/machines.html · #fig-5-1
FIG 5.1 · The simple DC motor in its maximum-torque position. The field exerts equal and opposite forces on sides AB and CD; sides BC and DA, parallel to the field, experience no force. The split-ring commutator and brushes carry current into the rotating coil and reverse it every half revolution.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three-dimensional perspective drawing, black and white. N pole block at left, S pole block at right, three horizontal field arrows from N to S across the gap, labelled B. Rectangular coil ABCD on a horizontal axle (dashed line labelled "axis of rotation"), coil plane horizontal so it is parallel to B. Sides AB and CD run parallel to the axle, one on each side of it (perpendicular to the field arrows); sides BC and DA run parallel to the field. Current arrows along the coil in the order A to B to C to D, labelled I. On the axle: split-ring commutator drawn as two half-cylinder segments with a visible gap (gap plane vertical), each segment joined to one end of the coil; two stationary brushes contacting the segments, wired to a battery with + and − terminals labelled. Force arrows: F on AB vertically upward, F on CD vertically downward, equal shaft lengths, identical arrowheads; callout "zero force — current parallel to B" pointing at sides BC and DA. Curved arrow around the axle showing the rotation sense consistent with the two forces. Equal aspect ratio; primary labels at minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; only shaft length conveys magnitude.
FIG 5.2 pass2/machines.html · #fig-5-2
FIG 5.2 · The Australian Synchrotron as the full VCE accelerator model: uniform electric fields increase speed in the linac; uniform magnetic fields change direction in the booster and storage rings, with the booster field ramped up as the electrons' momentum grows.
artwork spec (for reference)
Plan-view schematic, black and white, labelled "not drawn to scale". Left: small box labelled "electron gun" feeding a straight linac channel containing three pairs of accelerating electrodes; straight arrows between each electrode pair labelled "uniform E — speed increases, W = qV". Linac feeds a small circle labelled "booster ring — B ramped up as momentum grows, holding r constant". Transfer line to a larger circle labelled "storage ring — circumference 216 m, electron energy 3.0 GeV". Draw the storage ring as straight sections joined by eight wedge symbols for bending magnets; label one wedge "bending magnet: uniform B — direction change, r = mv/qB" and key the rest. Two tangent lines leaving the storage ring labelled "beamlines — synchrotron light to experiment stations". Electron-path arrows along the entire route. Identical arrowhead sizes; primary labels at minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, not raw superscript glyphs.
Fields and interactionsPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos2-interactions.html
FIG 1.1 pass3/u3aos2-interactions.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · Two identical bar magnets with north poles facing across a 4.0 cm gap; P is the midpoint. The two fields at P oppose and the net field is close to zero.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white. Two identical horizontal bar magnets drawn as rectangles on a common axis, separated by a gap labelled "4.0 cm" with a double-headed dimension arrow above the gap. Left magnet labelled S (left half) and N (right half); right magnet labelled N (left half) and S (right half), so the two N faces oppose across the gap. A dot at the exact midpoint of the gap labelled P. No field lines drawn. Caption note "Not drawn to scale". Pole labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 1.2 pass3/u3aos2-interactions.html · #fig-1-2
FIG 1.2 · A bar magnet, printed without field lines — the stimulus for the drawing task above.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white. A single horizontal bar magnet drawn as a rectangle, centred, with the left half labelled N and the right half labelled S in large type (at least 22 pt at authoring scale). Generous clear space on all sides — at least one magnet length above, below, left and right — so a student can draw field lines onto the figure. No field lines printed, no other annotation.
FIG 1.3 pass3/u3aos2-interactions.html · #fig-1-3
FIG 1.3 · Lucia's solenoid. Viewed from the left-hand end, the conventional current circulates clockwise.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white. A solenoid of about eight visible turns wound on a horizontal tube, connected by two leads to a battery drawn below; mark the battery's positive terminal with a long line and a + label. Current-direction arrows on both leads and on the front of two turns, consistent with the current circulating clockwise when viewed from the left-hand end. An eye symbol with an arrow at the left, labelled "view from this end". Ends labelled "left-hand end" and "right-hand end". No field lines. Labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale; identical arrowhead sizes throughout.
Effects of fieldsPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos2-effects.html
FIG 2.1 pass3/u3aos2-effects.html · #fig-2-1
FIG 2.1 · The charge arrangement for this question: equal positive charges due north and due east of point P, with a compass rose for stating the direction of the resultant field.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, equal aspect ratio. Point P as a small open circle labelled P at lower left. Charge A: circle containing a + sign, directly above P, labelled "A, +1.50 μC"; vertical separation marked "3.00 cm" with a double-headed dimension arrow placed to the left of the line AP. Charge B: identical circle with + sign, directly to the right of P, labelled "B, +1.50 μC"; horizontal separation marked "3.00 cm" with a dimension arrow below the line PB. Compass rose at top right: four arrows labelled N (up), E (right), S (down), W (left). No field lines drawn — the student must reason the directions. All labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 2.2 pass3/u3aos2-effects.html · #fig-2-2
FIG 2.2 · Gravitational force on the 500 kg probe against distance from Earth's centre. The shaded area under the curve between the two distances is the energy the question asks for; the grid is there to be counted.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white graph, left and bottom spines only. Vertical axis "force (N)" from 0 to 2000, ticks and gridlines every 250 N. Horizontal axis "distance from Earth's centre (×10⁷ m)" from 1.0 to 3.0, ticks and gridlines every 0.25. Full visible grid (students count squares). Smooth inverse-square curve through (1.0, 1990), (1.25, 1270), (1.5, 885), (2.0, 500), (2.5, 320), (3.0, 220) — no visible data points, clean line. Light grey semi-transparent shading under the curve between r = 1.0 × 10⁷ m and 3.0 × 10⁷ m, labelled "area = ΔE_g". Axis exponents typeset with maths formatting, not raw Unicode superscripts. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 2.3 pass3/u3aos2-effects.html · #fig-2-3
FIG 2.3 · Jo's model: an electron gun (parallel plates, 1.80 kV across a 25 mm gap) feeding a region of uniform magnetic field directed into the page. The diagram is not drawn to scale.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white schematic, not to scale. Left: two vertical plates; left plate labelled "−", right plate labelled "+" with a small central hole; supply symbol between them labelled "1.80 kV"; plate separation dimensioned "25 mm" with a double-headed arrow outside the gap; a dashed horizontal electron path from the left plate through the hole, labelled "electron, from rest". Right: a rectangular shaded region filled with evenly spaced × symbols and labelled "B = 2.0 mT"; a key "× = field into page". At entry to the region: velocity arrow v pointing right (east on the page) and force arrow F pointing down the page, arrowhead sizes identical. Dashed circular arc curving downward from the entry point, radius marked r to the arc's centre. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
Application of field conceptsPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos2-applications.html
FIG 3.1 pass3/u3aos2-applications.html · #fig-3-1
FIG 3.1 · The only force acting on a satellite in a stable circular orbit is the gravitational force exerted on it by Earth. The velocity is tangent to the orbit; the force is perpendicular to it at every instant.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white schematic, equal aspect ratio so the orbit is a true circle. Earth as a circle at centre (light continental hatching only); dashed circular orbit; satellite as a small rectangle on the orbit at the upper right. Two arrows from the satellite with identical arrowheads: a radial arrow toward Earth’s centre labelled “F_g — force exerted on satellite by Earth” and a tangential arrow labelled “v”, drawn exactly 90° apart with a small right-angle mark between them. Beneath the main panel, two small crossed-out insets labelled “NOT correct”: (1) the same satellite with an added forward arrow labelled “thrust”; (2) the satellite with an added outward arrow labelled “outward force” balancing F_g. Primary labels at minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; subscripts typeset with maths formatting, not raw glyphs.
FIG 3.2 pass3/u3aos2-applications.html · #fig-3-2
FIG 3.2 · The examinable DC motor diagram: coil JKLM in its maximum-torque position, plane parallel to the uniform field. Sides JK and LM feel the constant force pair; the split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three-dimensional perspective drawing, black and white. N pole block at left, S pole block at right, three horizontal field arrows from N to S labelled B. Rectangular coil JKLM on a horizontal axle (dashed line labelled “axis of rotation”), coil plane horizontal (parallel to B). Sides JK and LM parallel to the axle on opposite sides of it; sides KL and MJ parallel to the field arrows. Corner letters J, K, L, M at the coil corners. Current arrows in the order J to K to L to M, labelled I. Split-ring commutator on the axle: two half-cylinder segments with visible gaps (gap plane vertical in this position), each segment connected to one coil end; two stationary brushes contacting the segments, wired to a DC supply with + and − terminals labelled. Force arrows with identical arrowheads and equal shaft lengths: F on JK vertically upward, F on LM vertically downward; position the corner letters and current arrows so that the right-hand slap rule applied to each side gives exactly these force directions; callout “zero force — current parallel to B” pointing to sides KL and MJ. Curved arrow around the axle showing the rotation sense consistent with the force pair. Equal aspect ratio; primary labels at minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 3.3 pass3/u3aos2-applications.html · #fig-3-3
FIG 3.3 · The current balance. The magnet assembly sits on an electronic balance; the rigid wire between the poles is held by a separate stand. When current flows, the field exerts an upward force on the wire and — by Newton’s third law — the wire exerts an equal downward force on the magnets, pressing the assembly onto the balance.
artwork spec (for reference)
Side-view apparatus schematic, black and white, annotated “not drawn to scale”. Bench as a solid ground line with hatching below (heaviest line weight). Electronic balance as a rectangle on the bench with a display reading “0.00 g”. On the balance pan: a U-shaped steel yoke holding two slab magnets facing each other across a gap, pole faces labelled N (left) and S (right), three equispaced field arrows from N to S across the gap labelled B. A single straight rigid wire passing horizontally through the gap, perpendicular to the field arrows, clamped at both ends to a retort stand standing on the bench beside the balance; annotation “wire does not touch balance or magnets”. Double-headed dimension arrow below the pole gap, outside the structure, labelled “l = 5.0 cm”. Circuit from the wire ends drawn with standard symbols: variable DC power supply, ammeter A, switch. Current-direction arrow on the wire labelled I, oriented so the force on the wire is vertically upward. Two force arrows with identical arrowheads: upward arrow at the wire labelled “F on wire by field” and downward arrow on the magnet assembly labelled “F on magnets by wire — Newton’s third law pair”. Medium line weight for arrows; primary labels at minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, not raw superscript glyphs.

Unit 3 · AOS 3 — Electricity

The accident that lit the worldPass 1 · Big picture · pass1/electricity.html
FIG 1.1 pass1/electricity.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · The journey of energy from a spinning coil in the Latrobe Valley to a 230 V wall socket in Melbourne. Every voltage change along the way is a transformer — Faraday's law with no moving parts.
artwork spec (for reference)
Single wide panel, black and white, landscape, energy flowing left to right; equal aspect ratio; every voltage in the caption and stem shown on the diagram. Five stations joined by a continuous double line representing the conductors, with one bold open arrow beneath the full width labelled "energy flow". Station 1 (far left), labelled "generator — Latrobe Valley": rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles labelled N and S, curved rotation arrow around the axle, small sine-wave glyph at the output terminals, voltage label "20 kV AC". Station 2, labelled "step-up transformer": closed rectangular iron core with a primary coil of few turns on the left leg and a secondary coil of visibly more turns on the right leg; voltage labels "20 kV in" and "500 kV out"; sub-label "voltage up, current down". Station 3 (centre, widest), labelled "500 kV transmission lines — about 170 km": two lattice pylons with conductors sagging between them; sub-label "small current, small heating loss". Station 4, labelled "step-down in stages": two smaller transformer icons in series, each with more turns on the left leg than the right; voltage chain "500 kV → 66 kV → 230 V". Station 5 (far right), labelled "home — 230 V rms, 50 Hz": simple house outline containing a lamp symbol and a switch symbol. Line-weight hierarchy: heaviest for conductors and station outlines, medium for arrows, lightest for sub-labels; identical arrowhead size throughout. Footnote: "Not drawn to scale." Primary labels at least 22 pt sans-serif at authoring scale; no raw Unicode superscripts in rendered labels; check legibility at one-third scale.
9 · Electromagnetic inductionPass 2 · Core · pass2/induction.html
FIG 6.1 pass2/induction.html · #fig-6-1
FIG 6.1 · One loop, one field, three orientations. The flux is maximal when the field is perpendicular to the loop’s plane, zero when the field lies parallel to the plane, and between these values at intermediate angles — where only a qualitative statement is required.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three side-by-side panels sharing one uniform horizontal magnetic field drawn as equispaced parallel field lines, arrowheads pointing right, annotated "B = 0.10 T" once above the lines. In each panel the same rigid rectangular loop of area A = 2.0 × 10⁻² m², drawn in light perspective, with its area vector shown as a short dashed arrow normal to the loop's plane. Panel (a): plane of the loop perpendicular to the field (window faces the field; every line in its path threads it); label "Φ_B = B⊥A = 0.10 × 2.0 × 10⁻² = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb (maximum)". Panel (b): loop rotated about a vertical axis by roughly 50° so fewer lines pass through the window; label "flux between 0 and 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb — qualitative only in VCE". Panel (c): plane of the loop parallel to the field (edge-on; no lines pass through); label "Φ_B = 0". Draw lines that thread the loop in a heavier weight than lines that miss it. Black and white only; all arrowheads identical in size; typeset exponents with maths formatting (e.g. $^{-3}$), never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale and legible at one-third scale.
FIG 6.2 pass2/induction.html · #fig-6-2
FIG 6.2 · Flux and emf as a magnet falls through a coil. The emf graph is the scaled negative gradient of the flux graph: zero at the instant of maximum flux, opposite in sign on the way out, and larger on exit because the accelerating magnet changes the same flux in less time.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two graphs stacked vertically, sharing one time axis "time t (s)", left and bottom spines only, no gridlines, no data points. Top graph: vertical axis "magnetic flux Φ_B (Wb)"; one smooth asymmetric bump starting and ending at zero — rising as the falling magnet approaches and enters the coil, peaking when the magnet is centred in the coil, then falling back to zero noticeably more steeply than it rose (the magnet accelerates under gravity, so it exits faster than it entered). Bottom graph, aligned with an identical time scale: vertical axis "induced emf ε (V)"; the negative gradient of the top curve — a broad, shallow pulse of one sign while the flux rises, a zero crossing exactly at the instant of peak flux, then a taller, narrower pulse of the opposite sign while the flux falls. One dashed vertical reference line through both graphs at the instant of peak flux, annotated "flux maximum → gradient zero → ε = 0". Annotate the first pulse "magnet entering: flux changing slowly" and the second "magnet leaving faster: same ΔΦ_B in less time → larger emf". Shade both pulse areas light grey, semi-transparent, annotated "equal areas: N × the same total flux change". Small inset sketch at top left: a bar magnet (N pole downward) falling along the axis of a vertical coil whose ends connect to an oscilloscope. Black and white; identical arrowhead sizes; typeset all sub- and superscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
10 · Generators and alternatorsPass 2 · Core · pass2/generators.html
FIG 7.1 pass2/generators.html · #fig-7-1
FIG 7.1 · The alternator and the DC generator are the same machine — coil, magnet, axle, brushes — differing only in the rings that connect the rotating coil to the stationary circuit: two separate slip rings, or one ring split into two halves.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two side-by-side three-dimensional cutaway drawings, black and white, equal aspect ratio, labelled "(a) alternator" and "(b) DC generator". Each drawing: N pole block at left, S pole block at right, three horizontal field arrows from N to S labelled B; rectangular coil on a horizontal axle (dashed line labelled "axis of rotation"); curved arrow around the axle labelled "driven by external torque". In (a): two separate continuous metal rings side by side on the axle, each ring connected to one end of the coil, one stationary carbon brush pressing on each ring, leads to terminals labelled "AC output"; callout "slip rings — two separate rings, one per coil end; connections never swap". In (b): the identical machine but with a single cylinder split into two half-ring segments separated by visible insulating gaps, each segment connected to one end of the coil, two stationary brushes, terminals labelled "DC output"; callout "split-ring commutator — brushes swap segments every half revolution"; annotate that the gaps pass under the brushes when the plane of the coil is perpendicular to B (the zero-emf instant). Label in both: coil, axle, brush, N, S, B. All arrowheads identical in size. Add "Not drawn to scale." Minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale; typeset any subscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 7.2 pass2/generators.html · #fig-7-2
FIG 7.2 · The same coil, the same rotation — different rings. Slip rings pass the coil’s sinusoidal emf to the terminals unaltered; a split-ring commutator swaps the connections at every zero of the emf, flipping the negative half-cycles upward into varying DC.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two emf–time graphs stacked vertically, sharing the same time axis and identical vertical scales; left and bottom spines only. Time axis "time (s)" with ticks at 0, T/2, T, 3T/2, 2T; vertical axes "emf (V)" with ticks at +V_p and −V_p. Top graph titled "slip rings (alternator)": clean sinusoid starting at zero and rising, peaks +V_p at T/4 and 5T/4, troughs −V_p at 3T/4 and 7T/4, zeros at 0, T/2, T, 3T/2, 2T; bracket one full period labelled "T = one revolution". Bottom graph titled "split-ring commutator (DC generator)": the full-wave-rectified version of the same curve — identical positive humps peaking +V_p at T/4, 3T/4, 5T/4, 7T/4, touching zero at 0, T/2, T, 3T/2, 2T; annotation "polarity never reverses — magnitude still varies" and "two humps per revolution". Dashed vertical alignment lines through both graphs at t = 0, T/2, T, 3T/2, 2T labelled once "brushes swap halves at each zero of the emf". No data points; clean lines; typeset all subscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; labels legible at one-third scale.
FIG 7.3 pass2/generators.html · #fig-7-3
FIG 7.3 · One revolution of the coil set against the flux and emf graphs. The emf peaks where the flux is zero and changing fastest, and is zero where the flux is a maximum — a quarter-cycle offset — and the trace carries every measurable quantity: amplitude, peak-to-peak voltage, period and frequency.
artwork spec (for reference)
Triple-decker figure sharing one time axis from t = 0 to t = T. Top row: five end-on snapshots of the coil (drawn edge-on as a short bar between N pole at left and S pole at right, horizontal field arrows labelled B) at t = 0, T/4, T/2, 3T/4, T. Orientations: at t = 0 the plane of the coil is perpendicular to B (maximum flux); at T/4 parallel to B (zero flux); at T/2 perpendicular again (maximum flux, opposite face); at 3T/4 parallel; at T as at the start. Middle graph: magnetic flux Φ_B against time — cosine starting at +Φ_max, zero at T/4 and 3T/4, minimum −Φ_max at T/2; axis "magnetic flux (Wb)". Bottom graph: emf against time — sine starting at zero, peak +V_peak at T/4, zero at T/2, trough −V_peak at 3T/4; axis "emf (V)". Dashed vertical lines at each quarter period connecting snapshot to flux value to emf value. Callouts: at t = 0 "flux maximum, gradient zero — emf zero"; at t = T/4 "flux zero, changing fastest — emf maximum". Horizontal bracket from the flux peak at t = 0 to the emf peak at t = T/4 labelled "quarter of a cycle". On the emf graph: vertical arrow from 0 to +V_peak labelled "amplitude V_peak"; full-span bracket from −V_peak to +V_peak labelled "V_p–p = 2V_peak"; horizontal bracket over one cycle labelled "period T (one revolution), f = 1/T". Left and bottom spines only, no data points, equal aspect handling, all subscripts typeset with maths formatting; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
11 · Transformers and transmissionPass 2 · Core · pass2/transmission.html
FIG 8.1 pass2/transmission.html · #fig-8-1
FIG 8.1 · A transformer is two coils on one iron core. Alternating current in the primary drives an alternating flux around the core, and the changing flux through the secondary induces the output EMF. With fewer secondary turns than primary turns, this transformer steps 230 V RMS down to 11.5 V RMS.
artwork spec (for reference)
Schematic of the step-down transformer from the worked example. Closed rectangular soft-iron core drawn as a thick-outlined rectangular frame. Left limb: primary coil as a column of many loops labelled "primary coil, N1 = 600 turns", its two leads running to an AC source symbol (circle containing one sine cycle) labelled "V1 = 230 V RMS"; mark I1 with an arrow on a lead. Right limb: secondary coil with visibly fewer loops labelled "secondary coil, N2 = 30 turns", leads to a resistor symbol labelled "load, V2 = 11.5 V RMS"; mark I2 with an arrow. Around the core, a dashed flux loop with arrowheads labelled "alternating flux Φ_B guided by the iron core". Central annotation between the limbs: "no electrical connection — only the changing flux links the coils". Black and white, equal aspect ratio, all primary labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale; typeset all subscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 8.2 pass2/transmission.html · #fig-8-2
FIG 8.2 · The transmission system as one series loop: the supply voltage divides between the lines and the load. The voltage across the lines is Vdrop = IlineRline — never the full supply voltage.
artwork spec (for reference)
Circuit model of a transmission system with no transformers, using the first stage of the worked example. Left: AC supply symbol labelled "supply, V_supply = 500 V RMS". Two horizontal conductors run to the right; on the top conductor one resistor symbol labelled "R_line = 4.0 Ω (both lines lumped into one resistor)". Right: rectangle labelled "load (dairy), V_load = 340 V". Current arrow on the top conductor labelled "I_line = 40 A — the same current everywhere in the series loop". Bracket spanning the line resistor annotated "V_drop = I_line R_line = 160 V"; bracket spanning the load annotated "V_load = V_supply − V_drop". Below the circuit, a one-line power ledger: "P_loss = I_line² R_line = 6.4 kW · P_load = 13.6 kW". Black and white, line-weight hierarchy (conductors heaviest), labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale; typeset subscripts and superscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
Generation of electricityPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos3-generation.html
FIG 4.1 pass3/u3aos3-generation.html · #fig-4-1
FIG 4.1 · The flux through the rotating coil over two cycles, with blank emf axes beneath it sharing the same time scale. The sketch the question asks for is drawn on the lower axes.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white. Two sets of axes, vertically aligned and sharing the same horizontal scale; left and bottom spines only on each. Top axes: vertical axis "magnetic flux (×10⁻³ Wb)" from −2.4 to +2.4 with ticks at ±2.4 and 0; horizontal axis "time (ms)" from 0 to 80, ticks and light gridlines every 10 ms. Smooth cosine curve: +2.4 at t = 0, zero at 10 ms, −2.4 at 20 ms, zero at 30 ms, +2.4 at 40 ms, repeating to 80 ms; clean line, no data points. Bottom axes: vertical axis "emf (V)" with the zero line marked and no numerical ticks; horizontal axis "time (ms)" 0 to 80 with the same 10 ms gridlines; axes otherwise empty for the student's sketch. Axis exponents typeset with maths formatting, not raw Unicode superscripts. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 4.2 pass3/u3aos3-generation.html · #fig-4-2
FIG 4.2 · Anika's setup: a bar magnet, north pole downward, descending toward a horizontal coil. The viewing direction for the current answer is from above.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white schematic, equal aspect ratio. A flat circular coil drawn in slight perspective lying on a bench surface (ground line with hatch marks below); two leads from the coil to a circle labelled "A" (ammeter). Above the coil's centre: a vertical bar magnet, lower half labelled "N", upper half labelled "S", with a downward velocity arrow beside it labelled "v (constant)". A dashed vertical line from magnet to coil centre. An eye symbol above the apparatus with a downward sight line, labelled "viewed from above". No induced-current arrows drawn — the student must reason the direction. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 4.3 pass3/u3aos3-generation.html · #fig-4-3
FIG 4.3 · Shanna's generator: a rectangular coil rotating between magnet poles, connected to an oscilloscope through slip rings. Every value used in the question appears on the diagram.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white schematic, equal aspect ratio, not to scale. Centre: rectangular coil on a horizontal axle between two pole pieces labelled "N" (left) and "S" (right); field arrows from N to S across the gap labelled "B = 0.25 T"; coil dimensions marked with double-headed arrows outside the coil, "8.0 cm" along the long side and "4.0 cm" along the short side; annotation "40 turns" beside the coil; curved rotation arrow around the axle labelled "25 Hz". Right: two separate complete rings on the axle (slip rings), each with its own brush, labelled "slip rings" and "brushes"; leads to a rectangle labelled "oscilloscope". Labels split to opposite sides to avoid crowding; minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 4.4 pass3/u3aos3-generation.html · #fig-4-4
FIG 4.4 · Kenji's falling-magnet experiment and the recorded trace: two pulses of opposite polarity, the second larger and narrower.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, two panels. Left panel: vertical cardboard tube (open cylinder in outline); a coil of several visible turns wound around its middle, leads running to a rectangle labelled "oscilloscope"; above the tube a bar magnet with lower half labelled "N" and upper half labelled "S", downward arrow labelled "released from rest". Right panel: graph with left and bottom spines only, vertical axis "voltage (V)" with the zero line drawn, horizontal axis "time (s)"; trace flat at zero, then a broad shallow negative pulse labelled "A", a brief return to near zero, then a taller narrower positive pulse labelled "B", then flat at zero; no numerical values. Time axis starts at 0 and extends slightly past pulse B. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
Transmission of electricityPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u3aos3-transmission.html
FIG 5.1 pass3/u3aos3-transmission.html · #fig-5-1
FIG 5.1 · The alternator output on Shanna's oscilloscope at the original cranking rate. Every value the question asks for is read directly from the gridlines.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white graph with a full visible grid (students read values from it). Vertical axis "output voltage (V)" from −8.0 to 8.0, ticks and gridlines every 2.0 V, zero line slightly heavier. Horizontal axis "time (ms)" from 0 to 40, ticks and gridlines every 5.0 ms. A clean sinusoid with amplitude 6.0 V and period 20 ms, starting at zero and rising, completing exactly two cycles; no data points. Peaks touch the +6.0 V and −6.0 V gridlines exactly; zero crossings on the 10 ms gridlines. Axis labels outside the plot area; all labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 5.2 pass3/u3aos3-transmission.html · #fig-5-2
FIG 5.2 · The supply system: generator, step-up transformer, transmission lines with their total resistance, step-down transformer, town. Label the three voltages and the line current here before starting the algebra.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white schematic, left to right. Generator: circle with a sine symbol inside, labelled "generator, 200 kW, 4.0 kV rms". Transformer T1: standard two-coil symbol with core bars, labelled "T1 (ideal), N1 : N2 = 1 : 5", primary on the generator side. Transmission lines: two long parallel horizontal wires with a single resistor box drawn mid-span on the upper wire, labelled "lines, total resistance 40 Ω". Transformer T2: two-coil symbol labelled "T2 (ideal), 80 : 1", primary on the line side. Load: simple house outlines labelled "town". All five stem values must appear on the diagram. Medium line weight for wires, heavier for component outlines; labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; the diagram is not drawn to scale.

Unit 4 · AOS 1 — Light, matter, relativity

The light that would not behavePass 1 · Big picture · pass1/light-matter.html
FIG 1.1 pass1/light-matter.html · #fig-1-1
FIG 1.1 · The evidence board: three experiments that each broke a model. (a) Young's double slit — light through two slits makes many evenly spaced fringes, the signature of a wave. (b) The photoelectric effect — light ejects electrons from metal, but with energies a wave model cannot account for. (c) Electron diffraction — a beam of matter particles draws the ring pattern of a wave.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three equal square panels in a row, black and white, identical framing and equal aspect ratio, consistent line weights. Panel (a) "Young's double slit, 1801": small source at left labelled "monochromatic light"; vertical barrier with two narrow slits, separation labelled d; semicircular wavefront arcs spreading from each slit and overlapping in the region beyond; screen at right with the slit–screen distance labelled L; on the screen a vertical strip of at least seven alternating bright and dark bands, with adjacent bright bands marked "fringe spacing Δx". Panel (b) "Photoelectric effect, explained 1905": evacuated tube drawn as a rounded rectangle containing a metal plate at left labelled "metal surface" and a smaller electrode at right labelled "collector"; a wavy arrow entering the tube labelled "light, frequency f"; three straight arrows from plate to collector labelled "photoelectrons"; below the tube a circuit with an ammeter (circled A) and a variable voltage source labelled "variable voltage"; annotation line under the panel: "no electrons below the threshold frequency, however bright". Panel (c) "Electron diffraction, 1927": electron gun at left labelled "electron gun" emitting a straight beam arrow; thin vertical target in the beam labelled "thin crystal foil"; at right a circular screen face showing a central bright spot and at least three concentric bright rings, labelled "diffraction rings". Panel titles beneath each panel: (a) wave evidence, (b) particle evidence, (c) matter-wave evidence. All arrowheads identical in size across all panels. Footnote line: "Not drawn to scale." Primary labels ≥ 22 pt sans-serif at authoring scale; typeset subscripts and Δ with maths formatting, not raw Unicode glyphs; check legibility at one-third scale.
12 · Light as a wavePass 2 · Core · pass2/em-waves.html
FIG 9.1 pass2/em-waves.html · #fig-6-1
FIG 9.1 · A snapshot of an electromagnetic wave. The electric field oscillates in the vertical plane, the magnetic field in the horizontal plane; both are perpendicular to the direction of travel, and the two fields rise and fall together.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three-dimensional schematic. A straight horizontal axis arrow labelled "direction of travel (speed c)". The electric field: a sinusoid in the vertical plane along the axis, drawn with a solid line, with several vertical field-vector arrows from the axis to the curve, labelled "electric field E". The magnetic field: a sinusoid of the same wavelength and phase in the horizontal plane, drawn with a dashed line, with horizontal field-vector arrows, labelled "magnetic field B". Mark one wavelength λ between successive crests of the E sinusoid with a double-headed dimension arrow. Add a small right-angle symbol where the two oscillation planes meet to show E ⊥ B. Black and white; distinguish the two sinusoids by line style only (solid vs dashed) with a key "solid = E, dashed = B". All arrowheads identical in size. Typeset any exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale and legible at one-third scale.
FIG 9.2 pass2/em-waves.html · #fig-6-2
FIG 9.2 · The first three harmonics of a string fixed at both ends. Each harmonic fits one more half-wavelength between the same two nodes at the ends, so the allowed wavelengths are 2L, L and 2L/3 and the frequencies are f1, 2f1 and 3f1.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three stacked panels of equal width showing the same string of length L between two rigid clamps (vertical hatched anchors at left and right). Mark L once with a double-headed dimension arrow below the bottom panel. Each panel shows the standing-wave envelope: solid line for one extreme of the motion, dashed line for the other; filled dots labelled N at every node, open dots labelled A at every antinode. Panel 1 ("n = 1, fundamental"): nodes at the two ends only, one antinode at the centre, annotation "λ1 = 2L, f1". Panel 2 ("n = 2"): an additional node at the centre, two antinodes, annotation "λ2 = L, f2 = 2f1". Panel 3 ("n = 3"): nodes at 0, L/3, 2L/3 and L, three antinodes, annotation "λ3 = 2L/3, f3 = 3f1"; in this panel mark the node-to-node spacing with a small double-headed arrow labelled "λ/2". Key: "solid and dashed lines = the two extremes of the oscillation". Black and white, equal aspect ratio; typeset subscripts and fractions with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; labels legible at one-third scale.
13 · Diffraction and interferencePass 2 · Core · pass2/interference.html
FIG 10.1 pass2/interference.html · #fig-7-1
FIG 10.1 · The ratio λ/w decides the spread. A wide gap (λ/w ≪ 1) lets the wavefronts pass nearly straight; a gap comparable to the wavelength (λ/w ≈ 1) acts as a point source; a small obstacle is filled in behind. The wavelength itself never changes.
artwork spec (for reference)
Three equal panels, plan view, black and white. Each panel: plane wavefronts as parallel vertical lines approaching from the left, line spacing identical in all panels and labelled "λ" once per panel with a small double-headed arrow. Panel (a): barrier with a gap of width w ≈ 6λ (dimension arrow labelled "w"), heading "λ/w ≪ 1 — little spread"; beyond the gap the wavefronts continue nearly straight with slight curling only at the two edges; geometric shadow regions behind the barrier left blank. Panel (b): same barrier with gap w ≈ λ, heading "λ/w ≈ 1 — strong spread"; emerging wavefronts are concentric semicircles centred on the gap, spreading close to 180°; annotation "gap acts as a point source". Panel (c): no gap — a solid obstacle of width ≈ 2λ in the wave path, heading "obstacle comparable to λ"; wavefronts bend around both sides and rejoin behind it, annotation "waves fill in behind the obstacle". Common annotation beneath all three: "wavefront spacing (λ) unchanged by diffraction". Equal aspect ratio; identical arrowhead sizes; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 10.2 pass2/interference.html · #fig-7-2
FIG 10.2 · Geometry of the double slit. Light from S1 and S2 travels different distances to each point on the screen; the path difference at a point decides whether it is bright (nλ) or dark ((n + ½)λ). At the centre the paths are equal, so the central fringe is always bright.
artwork spec (for reference)
Plan view, black and white, annotation "Not drawn to scale — in practice L ≫ d". Left: vertical barrier with two slits labelled S1 (upper) and S2 (lower); slit separation marked with a double-headed dimension arrow labelled "d" outside the barrier. Right: vertical screen. Point C on the screen opposite the midpoint of the slits: straight lines S1C and S2C drawn equal, labelled "equal paths — path difference 0, bright (n = 0)". Point P higher on the screen: lines S1P and S2P, with the extra length of the lower path S2P marked by a short bold segment near S2 and labelled "path difference = S2P − S1P". Along the right edge of the screen, a vertical ladder of alternating bright and dark strips labelled top to bottom with their path differences: "bright 2λ", "dark 1½λ", "bright 1λ", "dark ½λ", "bright 0λ (central maximum)" at C, then mirrored below. Lines for paths medium weight, barrier and screen heaviest, labels split to opposite sides to avoid crowding; identical arrowhead sizes; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
FIG 10.3 pass2/interference.html · #fig-7-3
FIG 10.3 · The double-slit pattern on the screen. The fringe spacing Δx is measured centre to centre — between adjacent bright fringes or adjacent dark fringes, the value is the same — and grows with λ and L but shrinks with d.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, two stacked parts, annotation "Not drawn to scale — d is exaggerated; in practice L ≫ d". Top: side-on schematic of the apparatus — laser at left (labelled "laser, wavelength λ"), single slit, then double slit with separation marked "d" (double-headed dimension arrow outside the barrier), then screen at right; slit-to-screen distance marked "L" with a double-headed dimension arrow below the axis. On the screen: a vertical strip of evenly spaced bright bands (white) on dark background, central band marked "central maximum (n = 0)". Δx marked twice with identical short double-headed arrows: once between the centres of two adjacent bright bands, once between the centres of two adjacent dark bands, joint label "Δx — same value". Bottom: intensity-vs-position graph aligned under the screen strip, left and bottom spines only, axes labelled "intensity" (vertical) and "position on screen" (horizontal); evenly spaced equal-height peaks aligned with the bright bands; Δx marked between two adjacent peak centres with dashed drop-lines. Identical arrowhead sizes throughout; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
14 · The photonPass 2 · Core · pass2/photons.html
FIG 11.1 pass2/photons.html · #fig-8-1
FIG 11.1 · The photoelectric apparatus. Monochromatic light ejects electrons from a metal surface inside an evacuated tube; the variable, reversible supply either collects every electron (forward) or turns them back (reverse), while the ammeter reads the photocurrent.
artwork spec (for reference)
Schematic apparatus plus circuit, black and white, all components labelled, heavier line weight for the external circuit. A sealed glass envelope labelled "evacuated tube" containing two electrodes: a wide curved plate on the left labelled "metal surface (emitter)" and a small rod electrode on the right labelled "collector". Three parallel rays with arrowheads enter through the envelope and strike the plate, labelled "monochromatic light, frequency f"; a rectangle across the beam outside the tube labelled "filter selects one frequency". Dashed arrows from plate to collector labelled "photoelectrons". External circuit: wires from each electrode to a variable DC supply (battery symbol plus slider) labelled "variable voltage, polarity reversible", an ammeter circle A in series labelled "ammeter reads photocurrent I", a voltmeter circle V in parallel across the tube labelled "voltmeter reads V". Mark + and − terminals for the reverse (retarding) connection actually drawn, with the note "reverse polarity shown: the collector is negative, so the field opposes the photoelectrons". Minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 11.2 pass2/photons.html · #fig-8-2
FIG 11.2 · Photocurrent against potential difference for the same frequency at two intensities. Doubling the intensity doubles the saturation current, but both curves reach zero at exactly the same stopping voltage −V0: intensity does not change Ek max.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only, with the horizontal axis extended left of zero. Horizontal axis "potential difference across the photocell V (V)" from −2.0 to +4.0, tick at 0 and at −1.4; vertical axis "photocurrent I (μA)" from 0 to 5.0. Two curves of identical shape for the SAME frequency: solid curve (intensity 2I0) rising from zero at V = −1.4, climbing steeply through V = 0 and flattening to a saturation plateau at 4.0 μA by about V = +1; dashed curve (intensity I0) identical in form with its plateau at exactly 2.0 μA, also reaching zero at V = −1.4. Both curves meet the axis at the same point: mark it with a dashed drop-line labelled "−V0 (stopping voltage — same for both intensities)". Label the plateaus "saturation current ∝ intensity". Legend: solid = intensity 2I0, dashed = intensity I0, note "same frequency f". Clean lines, no data points, black and white; typeset exponents and subscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
FIG 11.3 pass2/photons.html · #fig-8-3
FIG 11.3 · Ek max against frequency for sodium and zinc. Every metal gives a straight line of gradient h; the horizontal intercept is the threshold frequency f0 and the extrapolated vertical intercept is −φ, so the two lines are parallel.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only, with the vertical axis extended below the horizontal axis to show negative intercepts. Horizontal axis "frequency f (×10¹⁴ Hz)" from 0 to 14, ticks every 2; vertical axis "E_k max (eV)" from −5.0 to +4.0, ticks every 1, so the sodium line (E_k max = 3.5 eV at f = 14 × 10¹⁴ Hz) stays inside the frame. Two straight PARALLEL lines, both of gradient 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s (0.414 eV per 10¹⁴ Hz on these axes). Line 1, solid, sodium (φ = 2.3 eV): crosses the horizontal axis at f0 = 5.6 × 10¹⁴ Hz, drawn solid above the axis and dashed below it, extrapolating back to the vertical intercept at −2.3 eV. Line 2, dash-dot, zinc (φ = 4.3 eV): horizontal intercept f0 = 10.4 × 10¹⁴ Hz, dashed extrapolation to vertical intercept −4.3 eV. Mark and label both horizontal intercepts "f0 (sodium)" and "f0 (zinc)" and both vertical intercepts "−φ (sodium) = −2.3 eV" and "−φ (zinc) = −4.3 eV" with dashed drop-lines. On the sodium line draw a rise–run gradient triangle between f = 8 × 10¹⁴ Hz and f = 12 × 10¹⁴ Hz annotated "gradient = h = 4.14 × 10⁻¹⁵ eV s — identical for every metal". Note on the figure: "dashed segments below the axis are extrapolations — negative kinetic energy is not physical". Legend distinguishing the metals by line style; black and white; typeset exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
15 · Matter wavesPass 2 · Core · pass2/matter-waves.html
FIG 12.1 pass2/matter-waves.html · #fig-9-1
FIG 12.1 · X-rays and electrons of the same wavelength passing through the same thin polycrystalline foil produce diffraction rings of the same dimensions — the evidence that matter propagates as a wave of wavelength λ = h/p.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two equal square panels side by side on a white background, each showing a circular diffraction pattern: a small dark central disc plus two concentric diffuse dark rings. Ring radii IDENTICAL in both panels (same centres, same radii, checkable by overlay). Left panel titled "X-rays, λ = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m"; right panel titled "electrons, λ = h/p = 2.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m".
