Above the panels, a small schematic: source → arrow → thin foil (thin rectangle labelled "polycrystalline foil") → arrow → screen. Shared annotation below both panels: "same wavelength → same ring radii". Black and white only; equal aspect ratio so rings are circular; add "Not drawn to scale." Typeset exponents with maths formatting (e.g. $^{-10}$), never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
FIG 12.2 pass2/matter-waves.html · #fig-9-2
FIG 12.2 · A single-electron double-slit pattern building up. Each electron arrives as one dot; only after thousands of arrivals do the dots reveal the interference fringes a wave of wavelength λ = h/p predicts.
artwork spec (for reference)
Four equal rectangular panels in a row, labelled beneath "N = 100", "N = 1000", "N = 10 000", "N = 100 000" (N = number of electrons detected). All dots identical small black points on white. Panel 1: ~40 dots, apparently random. Panel 2: faint vertical clustering. Panel 3: clear vertical bands. Panel 4: dense, sharply defined vertical fringes with empty dark gaps. Fringe positions and spacing Δx IDENTICAL across panels 2–4 and aligned column-to-column. Below panel 4, a small intensity curve (double-slit interference envelope) drawn with peaks exactly aligned to the dot bands, labelled "wave-model prediction". Black and white only; label N values in upright type; minimum 22 pt labels at authoring scale; typeset any exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs.
16 · Atoms and spectraPass 2 · Core · pass2/spectra.html
FIG 13.1 pass2/spectra.html · #fig-10-1
FIG 13.1 · Energy-level diagram for hydrogen, showing both labelling conventions, one emission transition and one absorption transition. Every spectral line of hydrogen corresponds to one pair of these levels.
artwork spec (for reference)
Vertical energy-level diagram. Left-hand scale "energy above ground state (eV)". Horizontal lines: n = 1 at 0 eV (heaviest line, labelled "n = 1 ground state"), n = 2 at 10.2 eV, n = 3 at 12.1 eV, n = 4 at 12.8 eV, n = 5 at 13.1 eV (lines converging toward the top, vertical spacing to scale), and a dashed line at 13.6 eV labelled "ionisation". Lightly shade the region above the 13.6 eV line and label it "continuous — free electron, any energy". Transition arrows: solid downward arrow from n = 3 to n = 2 labelled "emission ΔE = 12.1 − 10.2 = 1.9 eV" with a small wavy photon arrow leaving the level diagram to the right; solid upward arrow from n = 1 to n = 2 labelled "absorption 10.2 eV" with a wavy photon arrow arriving from the left. Right-hand scale labelled "alternative convention (eV)": mark 0 at the ionisation line and −13.6 at the ground state, with the annotation "ionisation = 0; bound levels negative — differences identical". All arrowheads identical in size; black and white only; typeset exponents and superscripts with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
FIG 13.2 pass2/spectra.html · #fig-10-2
FIG 13.2 · Emission and absorption spectra of hydrogen across the visible range. The bright emission lines and the dark absorption lines sit at identical wavelengths because both are set by the same level differences.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two horizontal spectrum strips, vertically stacked and sharing one wavelength axis below, labelled "wavelength (nm)" running 400 to 700 with ticks every 50 nm. Top strip labelled "emission — hot hydrogen gas viewed directly": solid black strip with four narrow white/bright vertical lines at 410, 434, 486 and 656 nm. Bottom strip labelled "absorption — white light passed through hydrogen gas (atoms occupying the n = 2 level, as in a star’s atmosphere)": continuous greyscale gradient strip (rendering the continuous spectrum in black and white) with four narrow dark vertical lines at the same wavelengths 410, 434, 486, 656 nm. Thin dashed vertical guide lines connecting each emission line to its absorption counterpart through both strips. Annotate the 656 nm pair "n = 3 ↔ n = 2, ΔE = 1.9 eV". Side note with small wavy-arrow sketch: "absorbed photons are re-emitted in random directions, so the forward beam is darker at these wavelengths". Black and white only; labels legible at one-third scale; typeset all numbers and units with maths formatting.
17 · Relativity and mass–energyPass 2 · Core · pass2/relativity.html
FIG 14.1 pass2/relativity.html · #fig-11-1
FIG 14.1 · The Lorentz factor against speed. γ remains close to 1 across the entire range of everyday and spacecraft speeds, then rises steeply toward infinity as v approaches c — which is why relativistic effects are negligible at low speeds and dominate near c.
artwork spec (for reference)
Graph, left and bottom spines only. Vertical axis "Lorentz factor γ" from 0 to 8, ticks at 1 through 8; horizontal axis "speed as a fraction of the speed of light (v/c)" from 0 to 1, ticks every 0.1. Single smooth curve γ = 1/√(1 − (v/c)²) starting at (0, 1.00) and passing exactly through (0.10, 1.005), (0.50, 1.15), (0.90, 2.29), (0.99, 7.09). Dashed vertical asymptote line at v/c = 1 labelled "γ → ∞ as v → c". Dashed drop-lines to both axes from the three points (0.50, 1.15), (0.90, 2.29) and (0.99, 7.09), each point labelled with its γ value. Light horizontal dashed line at γ = 1 labelled "γ = 1 at rest; γ is never below 1". Annotation with a small brace over the region v/c from 0 to about 0.4: "γ ≈ 1 — classical mechanics is an excellent approximation". No data points; clean line. Typeset all exponents and symbols with maths formatting (e.g. $^{2}$), never raw Unicode glyphs in rendered labels; minimum 22 pt primary labels at authoring scale.
FIG 14.2 pass2/relativity.html · #fig-11-2
FIG 14.2 · The same muon journey in two frames. Earth’s frame keeps the full 5.0 km of atmosphere but stretches the muon’s lifetime to 22 μs; the muon’s frame keeps the 2.2 μs lifetime but squashes the atmosphere to 5.0 × 10² m. Both frames conclude that the muon reaches the ground.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two side-by-side panels, black and white, equal aspect ratio, sharing a title strip "the same journey, two frames"; add "Not drawn to scale." Left panel "Earth frame": a vertical column of atmosphere between a cloud-top line labelled "muon created here" and a ground line with hatch marks below; double-headed dimension arrow outside the column labelled "L0 = 5.0 km (atmosphere at rest in this frame — proper length)"; a dot for the muon with a downward velocity arrow labelled "v = 0.995c"; annotation box "lifetime dilated: t = γt0 = 10.0 × 2.2 μs = 22 μs → range 6.6 km > 5.0 km — muon arrives". Right panel "Muon frame": the muon dot at rest at centre with the label "muon at rest — lifetime t0 = 2.2 μs (events of creation and decay both occur here — proper time)"; the atmosphere column drawn visibly squashed vertically with an upward velocity arrow on the column labelled "atmosphere moves upward at 0.995c"; dimension arrow labelled "L = L0/γ = 5.0 × 10² m"; annotation box "crossing time 1.7 μs < 2.2 μs — muon arrives". Identical arrowhead sizes throughout; vertical squashing of the right-hand column proportioned roughly 10:1 relative to the left. γ = 10.0 stated once between panels. Typeset all numbers, subscripts and exponents with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs; labels legible at one-third scale.
Light as a wavePass 3 · Detail · pass3/u4aos1-wave.html
FIG 4.1 pass3/u4aos1-wave.html · #fig-4-1
FIG 4.1 · Anika's string at rest between the two rigid clamps. The standing-wave pattern for part a is drawn onto this figure.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, equal aspect ratio. Two vertical rigid clamps drawn as heavy vertical bars with hatching on their outer sides, one at each end. Between them a thin horizontal dashed line representing the string at rest. The separation marked "1.5 m" with a double-headed dimension arrow below the string, placed outside the drawing space students will use. Generous clear vertical space above and below the dashed line for the student's drawing. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 4.2 pass3/u4aos1-wave.html · #fig-4-2
FIG 4.2 · Part of Jo's interference pattern. C is the central bright band; P is the third dark band to its right. The diagram is not drawn to scale.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white horizontal strip of alternating bright (white) and dark (black) vertical bands of equal width, at least nine bands visible. The central bright band labelled "C" with a leader line from below; counting rightward from C, the third dark band labelled "P" with a leader line from below. Bands evenly spaced; no measurements shown. Annotation beneath: "Not drawn to scale". Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
Light as a particle · matter as particles or wavesPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u4aos1-particles.html
FIG 5.1 pass3/u4aos1-particles.html · #fig-5-1
FIG 5.1 · The Ek max–frequency graph and its three readings: the gradient is Planck's constant, the x-intercept is the threshold frequency, and the extrapolated y-intercept is the negative of the work function. A metal with a larger work function gives a parallel line further to the right.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white graph, left and bottom spines only. Vertical axis "maximum kinetic energy E_k max (eV)" with the region below zero shown; horizontal axis "frequency (Hz)". Solid straight line for metal 1 starting on the x-axis at a point labelled f_0 (dashed drop-line to the axis), rising to the right; below the axis the same line continues as a dashed extrapolation to a y-axis intercept labelled "−φ". A rise-over-run triangle drawn against the solid line, labelled "gradient = h". A second dashed line, parallel to the first, crossing the x-axis further right, labelled "metal 2 (larger φ)", with its extrapolation reaching a lower y-intercept "−φ2". Legend distinguishing the two lines by line style. No numerical scales required. Exponents and subscripts typeset with maths formatting, not raw Unicode superscripts. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 5.2 pass3/u4aos1-particles.html · #fig-5-2
FIG 5.2 · Photocurrent against electrode potential for the same light frequency at full and half intensity. Halving the intensity halves the saturation current but leaves the stopping voltage untouched — intensity sets the photon arrival rate, not the photon energy.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white graph, left and bottom spines only. Horizontal axis "electrode potential (V)" spanning negative values through 0 to positive values, origin labelled 0; vertical axis "photocurrent" with no numerical scale. Two curves of identical shape: each rises from zero current at the same negative potential — labelled "−V0" with a dashed drop-line — climbs through V = 0 and flattens to a horizontal saturation plateau at positive potential. Solid curve labelled "full intensity" with the higher plateau; dashed curve labelled "half intensity" with a plateau at exactly half that height (verify the 2 : 1 plateau ratio visually). Both curves share the same intercept at −V0. Key for the two line styles. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 5.3 pass3/u4aos1-particles.html · #fig-5-3
FIG 5.3 · Diffraction rings from electrons (left) and from X-rays (right) passed through the same polycrystalline foil. The ring diameters match, so the wavelengths match — even though the photons carry about forty times the energy of these electrons.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, two square panels side by side at identical scale. Each panel: a dark background disc with a small bright central spot and two to three bright concentric rings; the ring radii IDENTICAL between the panels (use the same radii values for both). Left panel labelled "electrons", right panel labelled "X-rays", labels placed below each panel. A shared scale bar beneath the pair. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 5.4 pass3/u4aos1-particles.html · #fig-5-4
FIG 5.4 · Kenji's results: maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons against light frequency, with the line of best fit. The gradient, the x-intercept and the extrapolated y-intercept carry parts b and c of the question.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white graph with a full fine grid (students read values from it). Horizontal axis "frequency (×10^14 Hz)" from 0 to 10, major ticks and labels every 1, fine gridlines every 0.2. Vertical axis "E_k max (eV)" from −3.0 to 2.0, major ticks and labels every 0.5, fine gridlines every 0.1; the region below the x-axis is printed so the line can be extrapolated. Five data points as small crosses with vertical uncertainty bars of ±0.10 eV: (6.0, 0.25), (7.0, 0.60), (8.0, 1.10), (9.0, 1.40), (10.0, 1.85). Solid straight line of best fit passing through (5.5, 0) and (10.0, 1.9) — NOT through the first and last data points — continued below the x-axis as a dashed extrapolation meeting the vertical axis at −2.3 eV. Axis exponents typeset with maths formatting, not raw Unicode superscripts. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
Similarities between light and matterPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u4aos1-similarities.html
FIG 6.1 pass3/u4aos1-similarities.html · #fig-6-1
FIG 6.1 · Some energy levels of atomic hydrogen, with the ground state assigned 0 eV. Photon energies correspond to differences between levels, never to the printed energy of a single level.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white energy-level diagram. Four solid horizontal lines of equal width, vertical positions proportional to energy: 0 eV (bottom), 10.2 eV, 12.1 eV, 12.8 eV. One dashed horizontal line above them at 13.6 eV. Left-hand labels: "n = 1 (ground state)", "n = 2", "n = 3", "n = 4" beside the solid lines, "ionisation" beside the dashed line. Right-hand labels: "0 eV", "10.2 eV", "12.1 eV", "12.8 eV", "13.6 eV". A thin vertical axis arrow at far left labelled "energy (eV)" pointing upward. No transition arrows drawn — the questions require students to reason about them. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale; exponents typeset with maths formatting, not raw Unicode superscripts.
FIG 6.2 pass3/u4aos1-similarities.html · #fig-6-2
FIG 6.2 · Shanna's three-level model of the gas atom. Absorption in the cool gas starts from the ground state; emission from excited atoms can use every downward transition.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white energy-level diagram. Three solid horizontal lines of equal width, vertical positions proportional to energy: 0 eV (bottom), 2.1 eV, 3.5 eV. One dashed horizontal line at 5.0 eV. Left-hand labels: "ground state", "first excited state", "second excited state" beside the solid lines, "ionisation" beside the dashed line. Right-hand labels: "0 eV", "2.1 eV", "3.5 eV", "5.0 eV". Thin vertical axis arrow at far left labelled "energy (eV)" pointing upward. No transition arrows drawn. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
Einstein's special relativity · energy and massPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u4aos1-relativity.html
FIG 7.1 pass3/u4aos1-relativity.html · #fig-7-1
FIG 7.1 · The Michelson–Morley interferometer. One beam is split along two perpendicular arms of equal length and recombined; a difference in light's speed along the two paths would shift the interference pattern. None was ever observed.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white schematic, equal aspect ratio. Light source at left emitting a horizontal ray labelled "monochromatic light". At centre, a rectangle rotated 45° labelled "half-silvered mirror". Arm 1: ray continues right to a flat mirror labelled "mirror 1"; arm length dimensioned "L" with a double-headed arrow above the path. Arm 2: ray reflected vertically upward to "mirror 2"; arm length dimensioned "L" to the left of the path. Return rays recombine at the splitter and travel downward to a box labelled "detector (interference pattern)". Broad open arrow at top right labelled "supposed motion of Earth through the ether", pointing right. All rays solid lines with arrowheads in both travel directions; identical arrowhead sizes. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.
FIG 7.2 pass3/u4aos1-relativity.html · #fig-7-2
FIG 7.2 · The same muon journey in two frames. In Earth's frame the muon must cross 6.00 km but its lifetime is dilated; in the muon's frame its lifetime is 2.20 μs but the distance is contracted to 600 m. Both frames agree the muon arrives.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black and white, two side-by-side panels of equal size, equal aspect ratio. Left panel titled "Earth frame". Vertical scene: hatched ground line at bottom; a mountain profile with a small box labelled "detector, altitude 1.50 km"; a starburst high above labelled "muon created, altitude 7.50 km"; a straight downward arrow from starburst to detector labelled "v = 0.995c"; double-headed dimension arrow alongside the path labelled "L0 = 6.00 km (proper length, Earth frame)"; text label near the muon: "mean lifetime dilated: t = 22.0 μs". Right panel titled "Muon frame". The same scene vertically compressed to about one-tenth height: ground and mountain squashed; upward arrow on the mountain labelled "ground approaches at 0.995c"; dimension arrow labelled "L = 600 m (contracted)"; text label near the muon: "mean lifetime: t0 = 2.20 μs (proper time)". Identical arrowhead sizes throughout; line-weight hierarchy: heaviest for ground, medium for arrows, lightest for hatching. Labels minimum 22 pt at authoring scale.

Unit 4 · AOS 2 — The scientific investigation

The scientific investigationPass 1 · Big picture · pass1/investigation.html
FIG 15.1 pass1/investigation.html · #fig-15-1
FIG 15.1 · Accuracy and precision are independent. Each target shows where repeated readings fall relative to the true value at the centre. Precision describes how tightly the readings cluster; accuracy describes how close the cluster sits to the centre.
artwork spec (for reference)
Four identical circular targets in a single row, black and white, equal diameter, equal line weight. Each target is a set of four concentric rings drawn as thin solid circles, with a small solid dot marking the exact centre labelled "true value". On each target plot a cluster of five identical small filled marker crosses representing repeated readings. Target 1, labelled beneath "precise and accurate": the five crosses tightly grouped, all within the innermost ring, centred on the bullseye. Target 2, labelled "precise but inaccurate": the five crosses tightly grouped together but displaced as a group into the upper-right outer ring, well away from the centre. Target 3, labelled "imprecise but accurate on average": the five crosses widely scattered across all rings but distributed evenly around the centre so their mean position sits on the bullseye; add a small open-circle marker at the centroid labelled "mean". Target 4, labelled "imprecise and inaccurate": the five crosses widely scattered and offset as a group to one side. Beneath the row, two annotation lines spanning the full width: "spread of the crosses = precision" with a horizontal double-headed bracket over the scatter of target 4, and "distance of the cluster from the centre = (in)accuracy" with an arrow from the centre dot of target 2 to its cluster. All crosses identical in size; all labels sans-serif, primary labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale; verify legibility at one-third scale.
FIG 15.2 pass1/investigation.html · #fig-15-2
FIG 15.2 · Linearisation of a curved relationship. Raw data that curve (left) are replotted against a transformed independent variable (right) to give a straight line whose gradient corresponds to a physical quantity, read from two points on the line.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two square graph panels side by side, black and white, equal size, equal axis line weight, with a short right-pointing arrow between them labelled "transform the variable and its units". Left panel titled "raw data": vertical axis labelled "distance d (m)", horizontal axis labelled "time t (s)", both with tick marks and numeric scales. Plot six data points rising in a smooth upward-curving (concave-up) arc, each point a small filled marker carrying a short vertical uncertainty bar with end caps; draw a smooth freehand-style curve through the trend, clearly not a straight line. Right panel titled "linearised": vertical axis labelled "distance d (m)", horizontal axis labelled "time² t² (s²)" — emphasise the squared variable and the squared unit. Plot the same six points, now falling on a straight upward line, each with a vertical uncertainty bar; draw a single straight line of best fit passing within the bars and stopping at the last data point, not extended beyond. Mark two open-circle points lying exactly on the line, near the lower-left and upper-right ends, far apart, labelled "point on line"; from these construct a right-angled gradient triangle with a horizontal "run" leg and vertical "rise" leg, each labelled. Annotation beneath the right panel: "gradient = rise ÷ run, units = m ÷ s² = m s⁻²". Do not force the line through the origin; let it meet the vertical axis at a small positive intercept. All labels sans-serif, primary labels at least 22 pt at authoring scale; typeset the superscript 2 with maths formatting, not a raw Unicode glyph; verify legibility at one-third scale.
18 · The scientific investigationPass 2 · Core · pass2/investigation.html
FIG 15.1 pass2/investigation.html · #fig-15-1
FIG 15.1 · Linearising pendulum data. The curve of period against length becomes a straight line when period² is plotted against length; the gradient of that line equals 4π²/g.
artwork spec (for reference)
Two side-by-side black-and-white panels, equal size. Left panel: a curve of period (vertical axis, “period T (s)”) against length (horizontal axis, “length L (m)”) rising with decreasing slope (square-root shape), with five or six data points and small vertical and horizontal uncertainty bars on each. Right panel: the same data linearised, with vertical axis labelled “period² T² (s²)” and horizontal axis “length L (m)”; the points now lie along a straight line through approximately (0.20, 0.80) and (1.00, 4.0), each with vertical and horizontal uncertainty bars; a single line of best fit drawn through the scatter (not through the first and last points only, not forced through the origin), passing within the bars and stopping at the ends of the data. On the right panel, mark two widely spaced points on the line and show a rise/run construction (dashed right-angled step) labelled “rise” and “run” with the gradient annotation “gradient = 4π²/g”. A curved arrow between the panels labelled “square the period; relabel the axis s→s²”. Typeset all exponents and symbols with maths formatting, never raw Unicode glyphs in rendered labels; axis labels legible at one-third scale.
The scientific investigationPass 3 · Detail · pass3/u4aos2-investigation.html
FIG 15.1 pass3/u4aos2-investigation.html · #fig-15-1
FIG 15.1 · Linearised plot of distance against time² for a trolley released from rest, with a line of best fit, uncertainty bars, and a rise/run triangle drawn between two points on the line.
artwork spec (for reference)
Black-and-white line graph, square aspect. Vertical axis labelled "distance s (×10⁻² m)" with ticks 0 to 50 in steps of 10; horizontal axis labelled "time² (s²)" with ticks 0 to 1.0 in steps of 0.2. Six plotted points rising roughly linearly from near the origin, each with a short vertical uncertainty bar (±2 on the vertical scale) and a small horizontal uncertainty bar. A single straight line of best fit drawn through the trend, passing within all the bars and intercepting the vertical axis very close to the origin. Two open circles marked on the line, far apart (near x = 0.1 and x = 0.9), with a dashed right-angled triangle showing the rise (vertical) and run (horizontal) and the read-off coordinates labelled. No points forced; line not extended beyond the rightmost datum.

Batch editorial prompt

Copy this and paste it back to me to apply every change in one pass. Only figures with notes are included.
